notes.note: Gaius Trebonius, one of Caesar's assassins and governor of the province of Asia, was seized and murdered by Dolabella at Smyrna in early 43 BC; his death was both a personal loss and a strategic blow to the Liberators.

This is not Mark Antony but almost certainly Gaius Antonius, Mark Antony's brother, whom Brutus had captured in Macedonia; Brutus held him as a bargaining piece, aware that executing him might spark reprisals.

notes.anchor: Murcus, by Marcius, and by the army itself
notes.note: Lucius Statius Murcus and Quintus Marcius Crispus were Caesarian governors of Syria and Bithynia respectively who, along with their troops, declared for Cassius, giving him a commanding force in the East.

notes.anchor: my sister Tertia and to my mother
notes.note: Tertia (Junia Tertia or Tertulla) was Cassius's wife and Brutus's half-sister; "my mother" is Servilia, who dominated the Roman Republican women's network and could control the release of politically sensitive news.

The First Philippic delivered on 2 September 44 BC is sometimes numbered differently; the speech of 1 January 43 BC is Cicero's Fifth Philippic, the first major oration of the new consular year, urging war against Antony.

The name "Philippics" deliberately echoes Demosthenes' orations against Philip of Macedon; Cicero himself had used the term ironically in a letter, and Brutus here affectionately ratifies the now-famous label.
Publius Antistius Vetus, a well-connected senator and Brutus partisan, provided funds to Brutus's forces in Greece; vetus ("veteran" or "old friend") is an affectionate epithet, not a military rank.
Dyrrachium (modern Durrës, Albania), an Adriatic port and gateway to the Egnatian Road, was Brutus's principal base in Illyricum-Macedonia, dated here to 1 April 43 BC.
